semantic_description: A bar increase that creates two stitches from one by knitting into the front and then the back of the same stitch before sliding it off. Leaves a visible horizontal bar (purl bump) at the base of the second stitch. One of the most common and easiest increases. The visible bar makes it easy to count but less invisible than M1 variants.

written_forms: knit into the front of the next stitch, then without removing it from the left needle, knit into the back of the same stitch
knit front and back of next stitch
knit 1 front and back
knit into the front and back of the next stitch

drops: inc 1 by knitting front and back

ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'kfb'. Some older patterns use 'inc' or 'inc1' to mean kfb (since it was once the default increase). 'kfb' is now the universal standard. 'inc1' is ambiguous — could be any increase method.
'inc' or 'inc 1' alone is the oldest abbreviation and is highly ambiguous — it could mean kfb, m1, or any generic increase. Always check the pattern's stitch glossary.
Some patterns write 'inc in next st' which almost always means kfb.

rs: Knit the next stitch through the front loop as normal but do NOT slide it off the left needle
Keeping the stitch on the left needle, insert right needle into the back loop of the SAME stitch
Wrap yarn and knit through the back loop
Now slide the original stitch off the left needle — two stitches created from one

semantic_description: A double bar increase that creates three stitches from one by working into the same source stitch three times: knit through the front loop (do not drop), knit through the back loop (do not drop), then knit through the front loop again before dropping the stitch. Leaves two visible horizontal bars at the base of the new stitches. Produces a net increase of two stitches (+2). Common in top-down shawl construction where a single spine stitch is worked into three times on every right-side row to create paired increase columns on either side of a center stitch. Because all three resulting stitches come from one source stitch, the increase is balanced around that stitch with no lean.

written_forms: knit into the front of the next stitch but do not drop it, knit into the back of the same stitch but do not drop it, knit into the front of the same stitch again, then drop the stitch from the left needle
kfbf: [k1f, k1b, k1f] into the same stitch — 3 stitches made from 1
knit front, knit back, knit front of same stitch before sliding off needle
ambiguity_notes: kfbf is easily confused with kfb (knit front and back, 1→2) — a legend entry or OCR result that drops the trailing 'f' maps to the wrong stitch count entirely. Always verify the stitch count from the legend or pattern glossary.
kfbfb (1→4 stitches: front, back, front, back) is a further extension and a distinct operation — do not conflate with kfbf.
'k1fbf' is an uncommon but valid spelling used by some self-published designers.

rs: Insert right needle into the next stitch knitwise (front loop) and knit — do NOT slide the stitch off the left needle
Insert right needle into the back loop of the SAME stitch and knit — do NOT slide the stitch off the left needle
Insert right needle into the front loop of the SAME stitch again and knit
Now slide the original stitch off the left needle — three stitches have been created from one

semantic_description: The purl-side equivalent of kfb. Creates two stitches from one by purling into the front and then the back of the same stitch. Used on wrong-side rows to continue a bar increase line established by kfb on right-side rows.

written_forms: purl into the front of the next stitch, then without removing it, purl into the back of the same stitch
purl front and back of next stitch
purl into the front and back of the next stitch

drops: inc 1 by purling front and back

ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'pfb'. Direct purl equivalent of kfb.

rs: Purl the next stitch through the front loop as normal, do NOT slide off left needle
Rotate left needle slightly, insert right needle into the back loop of the same stitch from left to right
Wrap yarn and purl through the back loop
Slide original stitch off left needle

semantic_description: A nearly invisible increase that creates a new stitch by lifting the horizontal bar between two stitches from front to back and knitting it through the back loop. The resulting stitch leans to the left. Consumes no existing stitch — the new stitch is created from the inter-stitch bar. Paired with M1R for symmetrical shaping.

written_forms: lift the bar between stitches from front to back with left needle, knit through the back loop
make 1 left: insert left needle under the strand between stitches from front to back, knit through back loop
make one left-slanting increase
with left needle tip, pick up the strand between stitches from front to back and knit through the back loop
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'M1L'. 'ML' is used by some publishers (Purl Soho occasionally, Brooklyn Tweed). 'm1l' (lowercase) is equally common. Both cases should match to this operation.
When a pattern says just 'M1' without L or R, it USUALLY means M1L (left-leaning) in Vogue Knitting and most modern US patterns. But this is not universal — always check the glossary.

rs: Insert left needle from front to back under the horizontal strand between the stitch just worked and the next stitch
The strand is now on the left needle with the front leg on the right side
Knit this strand through the back loop (twisting it closed to avoid a hole)
One new stitch created; the increase leans to the left

semantic_description: A nearly invisible increase that creates a new stitch by lifting the horizontal bar between two stitches from back to front and knitting it through the front loop. The resulting stitch leans to the right. The mirror of M1L. Together they create symmetrical, nearly invisible shaping.

written_forms: lift the bar between stitches from back to front with left needle, knit through the front loop
make 1 right: insert left needle under the strand between stitches from back to front, knit through front loop
make one right-slanting increase
with left needle tip, pick up the strand between stitches from back to front and knit through the front loop
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'M1R'. Mirror of M1L.

rs: Insert left needle from back to front under the horizontal strand between the stitch just worked and the next stitch
The strand is now on the left needle with the back leg on the right side
Knit this strand through the front loop (twisting it closed to avoid a hole)
One new stitch created; the increase leans to the right

semantic_description: A generic make-one increase where the lean direction is unspecified. Lifts the bar between stitches and knits into it. When a pattern says just 'M1' without specifying left or right, the lean direction is ambiguous. Most modern US patterns default to M1L, but this is not universal. This operation exists to capture the ambiguous form.

written_forms: make one increase
pick up the bar between stitches and knit into it
insert left needle under the bar between stitches and knit into it
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'M1 or M1K' as make one stitch knitwise. 'M1' alone is ambiguous: it could be M1L, M1R, or either. When a pattern uses plain 'M1' without direction, it typically means M1L by convention (or the direction is inferred from position). Flag as ambiguous and note it could resolve to m1l or m1r.

This is the MOST ambiguous abbreviation in knitting. 'M1' can mean: (1) M1L (bar lift, knit tbl) — most common modern meaning; (2) KFB in some older patterns; (3) backward-loop cast on in some British patterns; (4) any generic increase. Always consult the pattern glossary.
If the pattern uses both 'M1' and 'KFB' separately, M1 almost certainly means the bar-lift variant.
If the pattern has separate M1L and M1R entries but also uses plain 'M1', it usually means 'use either, direction doesn't matter here.'

rs: Insert left needle under the horizontal strand between stitches
Knit into this strand (through front or back loop depending on desired lean)
One new stitch created

semantic_description: A make-one increase worked purlwise. Lifts the bar between stitches and purls into it. Used when the increase must be made on the purl side of the fabric (either on a WS row or within a purl section on RS). Creates a nearly invisible increase on the purl ground.

written_forms: lift the bar between stitches and purl into it
make 1 purlwise
pick up the bar between stitches and purl into it through the back loop
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'M1p' (make one purlwise). 'M1P' (uppercase) is common in patterns. Unlike M1L/M1R, the direction is often not specified with M1P — it typically means a generic purlwise lifted bar increase. Some patterns specify M1LP or M1RP for the purlwise directional form.

rs: Insert left needle under the horizontal strand between stitches from front to back
Purl this strand through the back loop to twist it closed

semantic_description: A generic increase symbol whose exact method is not specified. This represents a legend-level increase instruction rather than a named operation such as M1, KFB, LLI, or a backward-loop cast-on increase. It should be used when the source clearly indicates only 'increase' and leaves the technique to the legend or pattern notes.

written_forms: work an increase as specified in the legend
work an increase; see pattern legend for method
increase 1 stitch by the method described in the legend
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'inc' as a general term. 'inc1' is ambiguous (could be kfb). Only use this operation when the pattern explicitly leaves the method to the knitter's preference.

The abbreviation 'inc' alone is extremely ambiguous. In most pre-2000 patterns it almost certainly means KFB; in modern patterns it is rarely used without a method specification. Route 'inc' through this generic bucket and rely on context disambiguation.

rs: Follow the pattern or legend for the specific increase method

semantic_description: The standard single left-leaning purl increase, commonly abbreviated M1LP (Make One Left Purlwise). It is worked by lifting the strand between stitches from front to back and purling it through the back loop so the new stitch twists closed. This is the wrong-side equivalent of M1L and produces a left-leaning increase when viewed from the right side.

written_forms: make 1 left purlwise
lift bar from front to back, purl through back loop
make one left purl increase
work a left-leaning purl increase as specified in the legend
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'M1lp' (make one left purlwise). Both 'M1LP' and 'M1lp' are in active use. The order of letters (M1LP vs M1PL) varies by publisher — include both forms.
Both letter orderings (m1pl / m1lp) are in active publisher use. This operation is the single canonical target for all orderings.

rs: Insert left needle from front to back under the strand between stitches
Purl through the back loop

semantic_description: The standard single right-leaning purl increase, commonly abbreviated M1RP (Make One Right Purlwise). It is worked by lifting the strand between stitches from back to front and purling it through the front loop so the new stitch twists closed. This is the wrong-side equivalent of M1R and produces a right-leaning increase when viewed from the right side.

written_forms: make 1 right purlwise
lift bar from back to front, purl through front loop
make one right purl increase
work a right-leaning purl increase as specified in the legend
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'M1rp' (make one right purlwise). Mirror of M1LP.

Both letter orderings (m1pr / m1rp) are in active publisher use — CYC uses 'M1rp' while many English-language indie designers write 'M1RP'. This operation is the single canonical target for all orderings.

rs: Insert left needle from back to front under the strand between stitches
Purl through the front loop

semantic_description: A nearly invisible increase that creates a new stitch by lifting the left leg of the stitch below the next stitch on the left needle and knitting into it. Leans left. Unlike M1 which lifts the bar between stitches, LLI lifts a leg from the stitch in the row below. Produces an even more invisible result than M1 in some fabrics.

alternate_names: pick up and knit left leg below
written_forms: insert left needle into the left leg of the stitch below the next stitch on left needle, knit this lifted loop
lift the left side of the stitch in the row below and knit into it
knit the left leg of the stitch one row below the next stitch
insert needle into the left leg of the stitch directly below the next stitch on left needle and knit it
left lifted knit: pick up left leg of stitch below and knit
ambiguity_notes: Left lifted increase — a Purl Soho / Brooklyn Tweed favorite. Not in CYC master list. 'LLI' is the most common form. 'LL' is used by some designers (Joji Locatelli family). Distinguish from M1L: LLI lifts from 2 rows below the last stitch, M1L lifts from the horizontal bar between stitches.

rs: Insert the left needle tip from front to back into the left leg of the stitch directly below the next stitch on the left needle (one row down)
Knit this lifted loop
Then knit the next stitch as normal

semantic_description: A nearly invisible increase that creates a new stitch by lifting the right leg of the stitch below the stitch just worked and knitting into it. Leans right. The mirror of LLI.

alternate_names: pick up and knit right leg below
written_forms: insert right needle into the right leg of the stitch below the stitch just worked, knit this lifted loop
lift the right side of the stitch below the one just completed and knit into it
right lifted knit: pick up right leg of stitch just worked and knit
insert needle from front to back into the right leg of the stitch below the last completed stitch and knit it

rs: Insert the right needle tip from front to back into the right leg of the stitch directly below the stitch just worked (one row down, on the right needle side)
Place this loop on the left needle
Knit the lifted loop

semantic_description: A centered double increase that creates three stitches from one by working knit-purl-knit all into the same stitch. The center purl stitch creates a subtle vertical line. The increase counterpart to the central double decrease (CDD). Used for symmetrical shaping in shawls and lace.

alternate_names: knit purl knit in same stitch
written_forms: knit 1, purl 1, knit 1 all into the same stitch
(k1, p1, k1) into next stitch
work knit-purl-knit into the next stitch
(k1, p1, k1) into the next stitch
work (knit 1, purl 1, knit 1) all into the same stitch without dropping it until all 3 have been worked

vogue_knitting: k1, p1, k1 in next st

ambiguity_notes: This is a centered double increase (1-to-3). Often called 'CDI' in commentary but the abbreviation in legend is the instruction form. Purl Soho and Interweave use 'kpk' notation. Not in CYC.

rs: Knit into the next stitch but do not slide it off
Purl into the same stitch, still on the left needle
Knit into the same stitch once more
Now slide the original stitch off — three new stitches from one

semantic_description: A generic 1-to-3 increase that creates three stitches from one without committing to a single exact method. Patterns may realize this as knit-yarn over-knit, knit-purl-knit, through-the-back-loop variants, or other legend-defined expansions.

written_forms: increase from 1 stitch to 3 stitches
work a 1-to-3 increase as specified in the legend or pattern
work 3 stitches into the same stitch

drops: k1, yo, k1 into next st
patons: k into front and back and front of next st

ambiguity_notes: Multiple methods produce a 1-to-3 increase: kpk, k1-yo-k1, (k1p1k1). This generic operation covers all methods. The abbreviation in a pattern legend will usually specify the exact method — treat 'inc-1-to-3' as a fallback.

rs: Work the specific 1-to-3 increase method specified by the pattern
Do not assume a single default method without legend context

semantic_description: An exact centered 1-to-3 increase worked as knit 1, yarn over, knit 1 into the same stitch. It creates two knit stitches separated by a yarn-over eyelet and is more specific than a generic 1-to-3 increase or a generic centered increase.

written_forms: work knit 1, yarn over, knit 1 into the same stitch
(k1, yo, k1) into next stitch
knit 1, yarn over, knit 1 all into the same stitch
(k1, yo, k1) into the next stitch

drops: k1, yo, k1 in next st
interweave: k1, yo, k1 into next st

rs: Knit into the next stitch but do not slide it off the left needle
Make a yarn over
Knit into the same stitch again
Slide the original stitch off the left needle

semantic_description: An exact centered purl-ground 1-to-3 increase worked as purl 1, yarn over, purl 1 into the same stitch. It creates two purl stitches separated by a yarn-over increase and is more specific than a generic centered purl increase.

written_forms: work purl 1, yarn over, purl 1 into the same stitch
(p1, yo, p1) into next stitch
purl 1, yarn over, purl 1 all into the same stitch
(p1, yo, p1) into the next stitch

drops: p1, yo, p1 in next st

rs: Purl into the next stitch but do not slide it off the left needle
Make a yarn over using the purl-side yarn path required by the pattern
Purl into the same stitch again
Slide the original stitch off the left needle

semantic_description: Creates four stitches from one. Typically worked as (k1, p1, k1, p1) or (k1, yo, k1, yo) into the same stitch. Used in fan/shell patterns and some bobble setups.

written_forms: work (k1, p1, k1, p1) into the same stitch
increase from 1 stitch to 4 stitches
work (k1, p1) twice all into the same stitch
(k1, p1, k1, p1) into next stitch

drops: (k1, p1, k1, p1) into next st

ambiguity_notes: No standard publisher abbreviation. Internal convenience forms only.

rs: Work alternating knit and purl (or knit and YO) into the same stitch 4 times
Slide original stitch off

semantic_description: Creates five stitches from one. Commonly worked as (k1, yo, k1, yo, k1) into the same stitch. Used in bobble setup rows, fan/shell lace patterns, and dramatic increases. Often the first step of a bobble — the five stitches are then turned and decreased back to one over several short rows.

written_forms: work (k1, yo, k1, yo, k1) into the same stitch
knit 1, [yarn over, knit 1] twice into the same stitch
increase from 1 stitch to 5 stitches
(k1, yo) twice, k1 into the same stitch

rowan: k into front back front back and front
debbie_bliss: (k1, yo) twice, k1 all in next st

ambiguity_notes: 'MB' (make bobble) is the most common chart abbreviation for a 1-to-5 expansion, because the classic 5-stitch bobble begins by making 5 from 1. However 'MB' strictly means make bobble (which includes subsequent rows and the finishing collapse), not just the expansion. Treat 'MB' as likely mapping to the bobble operation, not to this bare expansion.
'mb' (make bobble) is sometimes the same as inc_1_to_5 for the setup row, but not always — always check the pattern's bobble definition.

rs: Knit into next stitch, do not remove
Yarn over
Knit into same stitch
Yarn over
Knit into same stitch
Slide original stitch off — 5 new stitches

semantic_description: Creates 6 stitches from one. Typically realized in the legend as a sequence of (k1, yo) repeats or alternating knit/purl into the same stitch — the exact technique is legend-defined. Used in fan/shell, feather-and-fan, and dramatic shell-pattern increases. The Stitchmastery 'Increase to 6 stitches' symbol family resolves to this operation.

written_forms: work an increase from 1 stitch to 6 stitches as specified in the legend
increase to 6 stitches in the next stitch
increase 1 to 6 stitches

rs: Work the legend-specified 1-to-6 expansion into the next stitch
Slide original stitch off the left needle

semantic_description: Creates 7 stitches from one. Typically realized in the legend as a sequence of (k1, yo) repeats or alternating knit/purl into the same stitch — the exact technique is legend-defined. Used in fan/shell, feather-and-fan, and dramatic shell-pattern increases. The Stitchmastery 'Increase to 7 stitches' symbol family resolves to this operation.

written_forms: work an increase from 1 stitch to 7 stitches as specified in the legend
increase to 7 stitches in the next stitch
increase 1 to 7 stitches

rs: Work the legend-specified 1-to-7 expansion into the next stitch
Slide original stitch off the left needle

semantic_description: Creates 8 stitches from one. Typically realized in the legend as a sequence of (k1, yo) repeats or alternating knit/purl into the same stitch — the exact technique is legend-defined. Used in fan/shell, feather-and-fan, and dramatic shell-pattern increases. The Stitchmastery 'Increase to 8 stitches' symbol family resolves to this operation.

written_forms: work an increase from 1 stitch to 8 stitches as specified in the legend
increase to 8 stitches in the next stitch
increase 1 to 8 stitches

rs: Work the legend-specified 1-to-8 expansion into the next stitch
Slide original stitch off the left needle

semantic_description: Creates 9 stitches from one. Typically realized in the legend as a sequence of (k1, yo) repeats or alternating knit/purl into the same stitch — the exact technique is legend-defined. Used in fan/shell, feather-and-fan, and dramatic shell-pattern increases. The Stitchmastery 'Increase to 9 stitches' symbol family resolves to this operation.

written_forms: work an increase from 1 stitch to 9 stitches as specified in the legend
increase to 9 stitches in the next stitch
increase 1 to 9 stitches

rs: Work the legend-specified 1-to-9 expansion into the next stitch
Slide original stitch off the left needle

semantic_description: Creates a new stitch mid-row by casting on using the backward loop method. Twists the working yarn into a loop and places it on the right needle. The simplest possible increase — no stitch is consumed. Used for adding stitches at the end of a row or mid-row when other increases are impractical. Produces a loose stitch that tends to gap.

written_forms: cast on 1 stitch using backward loop method
make a backward loop and place on right needle
using the backward loop method, cast on 1 stitch
make a backward loop with the working yarn and place on right needle tip
e-wrap one stitch onto right needle

knitpicks: cast on 1 using backward loop method

ambiguity_notes: When used as an increase mid-row, this is often written simply as 'CO 1' or 'cable CO 1'. The 'backward loop' as a cast-on method and as a mid-row increase are mechanically identical; context (cast-on row vs. body row) distinguishes them.
In British vintage patterns 'M1' sometimes means the backward-loop cast-on increase. Do not conflate with lifted-bar make-one.
'cast on 1' mid-row instruction in Vogue Knitting always means the backward loop method unless otherwise specified.

rs: Wrap working yarn around left thumb (or index finger) to form a loop
Insert right needle into the loop from below
Slide loop onto right needle and tighten

semantic_description: A variation of purl front and back that creates two stitches from one, with the increase leaning in the opposite direction from KFB. The mirror-image bar increase.

written_forms: pass left increase
knit into the stitch in the row below and pass the stitch on the left needle over
ambiguity_notes: JIS (Japanese Industrial Standard) chart operation — a left-leaning bar increase worked from the back. 'pl' could collide with purl left in casual notation. Not a CYC term; only in Japanese chart convention contexts.
Outside Japanese patterns, this technique is rarely encountered under this name. Always verify against the pattern's JIS legend.

rs: Work into front and back of stitch with reversed orientation

semantic_description: A generic left-leaning increase symbol. Unlike an exact named method such as M1L or LLI, this entry represents a chart or legend item that specifies only the left slant of the added stitch. It is appropriate for sources that distinguish a generic left increase from specific make-one methods.

written_forms: work a left-leaning increase as specified in the legend
work a left-slanting increase as directed in the pattern legend
ambiguity_notes: Generic left-leaning increase placeholder. Should resolve to m1l, lli, or kfb per legend.

rs: Work the left-leaning increase method specified by the pattern or legend

semantic_description: A generic right-leaning increase symbol. Unlike an exact named method such as M1R or RLI, this entry captures sources that specify the lean direction but not the exact increase mechanics. It is the mirror-image counterpart to a generic left-leaning increase.

written_forms: work a right-leaning increase as specified in the legend
work a right-slanting increase as directed in the pattern legend
ambiguity_notes: Generic right-leaning increase placeholder.

rs: Work the right-leaning increase method specified by the pattern or legend

semantic_description: A left-leaning increase symbol annotated with a numeral or count mark. In StitchMastery's counted increase family, the numeric decoration indicates a multi-stitch or counted increase variant, but the exact resulting stitch count must be taken from the symbol variant or legend. This family is distinct from a plain left-leaning increase and from an exact method such as M1L.

written_forms: work the counted left-leaning increase specified by the symbol and legend
work left-leaning increase the number of times specified by the numeral in the chart symbol
ambiguity_notes: The 'N' in the abbreviation is a placeholder for a number. This is a Knitr-internal convention for parameterized operations. No standard pattern abbreviation.

rs: Read the numeral or count attached to the symbol
Work the left-leaning counted increase defined in the legend

semantic_description: A right-leaning increase symbol annotated with a numeral or count mark. It represents the right-leaning branch of StitchMastery's counted increase family and preserves the fact that the symbol is more specific than a plain right-leaning increase, even when the exact stitch count must still be taken from the variant or legend.

written_forms: work the counted right-leaning increase specified by the symbol and legend
work right-leaning increase the number of times specified by the numeral in the chart symbol
ambiguity_notes: Same as increase_left_counted. Knitr-internal.

rs: Read the numeral or count attached to the symbol
Work the right-leaning counted increase defined in the legend

semantic_description: A generic centered 1-to-3 increase. The symbol specifies that one stitch expands to three in a centered arrangement, but it does not force a single method such as KPK or k1-yo-k1. This makes it broader than a named central-double-increase technique while still more precise than a generic 1-to-3 increase.

written_forms: work a centered increase as specified in the legend
work a centered increase as specified by legend; common methods: kpk, k1-yo-k1
ambiguity_notes: 'CDI' (central double increase / centered double increase) is used by Interweave and some Ravelry designers. Not in CYC. Sometimes written 'CDI' in pattern body with the legend explaining the specific method (kpk, or k1-yo-k1).

rs: Work the centered 1-to-3 increase method specified by the pattern
Common realizations include knit-purl-knit or knit-yarn over-knit variants

semantic_description: A purl-ground or purlwise centered 1-to-3 increase. StitchMastery distinguishes this from a generic centered increase, so it should not be collapsed into KPK. It is the centered purl counterpart to a generic centered increase.

written_forms: work a centered purl increase as specified in the legend
work a centered purl increase as specified by legend; common method: p1-yo-p1
ambiguity_notes: The purl-side version of CDI. Not in CYC. Pattern-defined.

rs: Work the centered purl-ground increase method specified by the pattern

semantic_description: A generic increase worked through the back loop or crossed orientation. This captures chart or legend entries that specify a twisted/back-loop increase without fixing one exact method such as KFB through the back loop or a twisted lifted increase.

written_forms: work the increase through the back loop as specified in the legend
work an increase into the back loop of the stitch
ambiguity_notes: Generic twisted increase. Could be kfb-tbl or a M1-tbl variant. Pattern-defined.

rs: Work the specified increase through the back loop or crossed orientation

semantic_description: A source-specific chart symbol whose meaning is pattern-defined as either a slip or an increase. It should not be conflated with M1, because the symbol explicitly encodes ambiguity that must be resolved from the legend.

written_forms: work as slip or increase according to the legend
ambiguity_notes: This is an inherently ambiguous operation — the pattern uses a single symbol for two behaviors depending on position in shaping. No standard abbreviation. Cannot assign without knowing pattern context. Legend matching should flag this as requiring user confirmation.

rs: Consult the pattern legend to determine whether this symbol is worked as a slip or as an increase

semantic_description: A purlwise or purl-ground variant of the source-specific 'slip or increase' symbol. It remains intentionally ambiguous until the legend resolves whether the stitch is slipped or increased, so it should not be merged with M1P or any exact purl increase.

written_forms: work as a purlwise slip or purlwise increase according to the legend
ambiguity_notes: Same situation as increase_or_slip but purlwise. No standard abbreviation. Requires pattern-specific interpretation.

rs: Consult the pattern legend to determine whether this symbol is slipped purlwise or worked as a purlwise increase

semantic_description: Creates a new stitch by lifting the horizontal bar between two stitches and knitting it through the FRONT loop. Unlike M1L and M1R which knit through the back loop to close the stitch, this variant leaves a small deliberate hole or eyelet at the base. Used intentionally in some lace and textured patterns where a slight opening is desired. Also called 'open make-one'.

written_forms: lift the bar between stitches with left needle, knit through the front loop (leaving a small eyelet)
make one stitch by picking up the bar and knitting it through the front loop without twisting

stitchmastery: make one knitwise through front loop

ambiguity_notes: Make one through front loop — produces an open (untwisted) increase, unlike standard M1L/M1R which work through the back loop. Used by some designers who want a more visible eyelet. 'M1F' is a reasonable abbreviation but uncommon; some patterns just write 'M1' and rely on the front-loop instruction in the legend text.
Most modern patterns intend the twisted (tbl) variant when they write M1L or M1R. This is the less common open form.
When a pattern says only 'M1' in a lace context without L/R specification, this open variant may be intended — check the legend.

rs: lift the bar between stitches with left needle, knit through the front loop (leaving a small eyelet)
make one stitch by picking up the bar and knitting it through the front loop without twisting

semantic_description: A bar increase worked by knitting into the back loop first, then the front loop of the same stitch. This is the reverse of KFB. It produces two stitches from one but with a twisted first stitch and a different visual profile than KFB. Less common than KFB but appears in CYC-sourced patterns and some Vogue Knitting publications.

written_forms: knit into the back loop of the next stitch, then without removing it, knit into the front loop of the same stitch
k1 back and front of same stitch
ambiguity_notes: 'Knit back then front' — the reverse of kfb, resulting in a different bar placement. Used in Bavarian/German twisted stitch patterns and some modern designers for aesthetic reasons. Relatively uncommon. 'kb&f' distinguishes from 'kfb'. 'kbf' alone could be misread as 'kfb' by pattern OCR.

rs: knit into the back loop of the next stitch, then without removing it, knit into the front loop of the same stitch
k1 back and front of same stitch

semantic_description: A purl bar increase worked by purling into the back loop first, then the front loop of the same stitch. The purl equivalent of KBF. Rare but appears in CYC-sourced and Vogue Knitting patterns as a WS complement to KBF.

written_forms: purl into the back loop of the next stitch, then without removing it, purl into the front loop of the same stitch
ambiguity_notes: Purl equivalent of kbf. 'PBF' could collide with 'place bead' + 'front' instruction shorthands in some beaded knitting patterns.

rs: purl into the back loop of the next stitch, then without removing it, purl into the front loop of the same stitch

semantic_description: A German-tradition increase that lifts the bar between stitches and knits through the back loop without specifying a lean direction. Used in German publications (Lana Grossa, Schachenmayr, Woolly Hugs) and older continental European patterns where M1L/M1R directionality is not distinguished. Distinct from M1L/M1R: those are direction-specific; this is direction-neutral but always twisted.

written_forms: pick up the bar between stitches with the left needle and knit through the back loop
Masche aufnehmen und verschränkt abstricken
ambiguity_notes: Make one through back loop — produces a twisted stitch. Used in German/Bavarian twisted stitch work. 'M1tbl' is the most common English-language form. Distinct from M1L in that it twists the loop.

rs: pick up the bar between stitches with the left needle and knit through the back loop
Masche aufnehmen und verschränkt abstricken

semantic_description: The wrong-side or purl-ground equivalent of LLI. Creates a new stitch by lifting the left leg of the stitch one row below the next stitch on the left needle and purling into it. Used on WS rows to continue a lifted-increase shaping line established by LLI on RS rows. Less common than LLI and rarely has a dedicated chart symbol — appears primarily in written instructions.

written_forms: insert left needle into the left leg of the stitch below the next stitch on left needle and purl into it
purl the left leg of the stitch one row below the next stitch
ambiguity_notes: Purlwise version of LLI. Used on wrong-side rows. Not in CYC. Used by Purl Soho and similar designers.

rs: insert left needle into the left leg of the stitch below the next stitch on left needle and purl into it
purl the left leg of the stitch one row below the next stitch

semantic_description: The wrong-side or purl-ground equivalent of RLI. Creates a new stitch by lifting the right leg of the stitch one row below the stitch just worked and purling into it. Used on WS rows to continue a lifted-increase shaping line established by RLI on RS rows.

written_forms: insert right needle into the right leg of the stitch below the stitch just worked and purl into it
purl the right leg of the stitch one row below the last completed stitch

rs: insert right needle into the right leg of the stitch below the stitch just worked and purl into it
purl the right leg of the stitch one row below the last completed stitch

semantic_description: Increase one stitch into 3 stitches, worked from the WS. The purl counterpart of inc_1_to_3; when a 1-to-3 increase symbol appears on a WS row, this is the action worked.

written_forms: Work a 1-to-3 purl increase as specified in the legend.
ambiguity_notes: WS counterpart op. Generic; specific technique (yo / m1 / kfb variants) is legend-defined.

rs: Work the 1-to-3 purl increase per pattern legend.

semantic_description: Generic 1-to-2 increase: one stitch becomes two via a legend-defined method (kfb, m1 + knit, yo + knit, lifted, etc.). Shown on SM-style charts as a wide 2-cell arc connecting two stitches to one. The specific technique is given in the pattern legend.

written_forms: increase 1 stitch to 2 stitches using the method specified in the legend
ambiguity_notes: Generic 1→2 increase. Specific realizations include kfb, m1+knit, yo+knit, lifted-then-knit. The chart symbol (2-cell wide arc in SM) is the generic visual; the legend specifies which technique.
Distinct from kfb — kfb is a specific knit-front-and-back technique. Use this op when the symbol is the generic 2-cell arc and the legend doesn't specify kfb.

rs: Work the 1-to-2 increase using the method specified in the pattern legend.

semantic_description: Generic 1-to-2 purl increase — the WS counterpart of inc_1_to_2. The specific technique is legend-defined (pfb, m1p + purl, yo + purl, etc.).

written_forms: Work the 1-to-2 purl increase as specified in the legend.

rs: Work the 1-to-2 purl increase per pattern legend.
